gating_notes: renal disease status is yes/unknown (dose adjustment / avoid certain meds)
liver disease status is yes/unknown (avoid hepatotoxic meds / adjust dosing)

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 34-year-old female presenting with a 4-day history of burning sensation during urination localized to her genital area. She reports the symptom has remained consistent since onset and is of moderate severity. She also notes increased urinary frequency. She has not tried any treatments yet. She has a history of renal disease and is not pregnant. She has a penicillin allergy. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no vital signs or physical exam available.

A: 
- Working diagnosis (most likely): Acute uncomplicated cystitis 
- Differential: 
 1. Urinary tract infection (cystitis) 
 2. Urethritis (possibly infectious or irritant) 
 3. Bladder stones or other urinary tract pathology 
 4. Vaginitis or other genital infection 
- Red flags considered: No fever, flank pain, hematuria, or systemic symptoms reported.

P: 
 - Medication/treatment support (deterministic):
   - Plan assessment:
     - Working impression based on telephone history. Management below is limited by telephone-only evaluation (no vitals/labs available during the call).
   - Rx candidates:
     - Supportive care — symptom relief and supportive management
   - Gating flags:
     - Pregnancy: no
     - Renal disease: unknown
     - Liver disease: unknown
   - Return precautions:
     - Go to urgent care/ED now for severe or rapidly worsening symptoms, trouble breathing, chest pain, confusion, fainting, or inability to keep fluids down.
     - Seek urgent in-person care for new high fever, severe pain, dehydration concerns, or any red-flag symptoms discussed during the call.
   - Testing:
     - If no improvement within 48–72 hours, worsening symptoms, or recurrent symptoms: seek in-person evaluation for appropriate testing (e.g., urinalysis/urine culture or other workup as indicated).
- Disposition: Patient can be managed outpatient with close follow-up. If symptoms worsen or systemic signs develop, she should seek urgent evaluation. 
- Plan: Empiric treatment for uncomplicated urinary tract infection is appropriate given symptoms and history. Given her penicillin allergy, recommend nitrofurantoin or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole as first-line alternatives, pending renal function assessment due to her renal disease. Encourage increased fluid intake and symptom monitoring. Urinalysis and urine culture should be obtained if possible to guide therapy. 
- Safety net: Advise patient to seek immediate care if she develops fever, flank pain, worsening symptoms, or signs of systemic infection. If symptoms persist beyond 3 days of treatment or worsen, she should return for reassessment.

evidence.title: Burn Evaluation: MedlinePlus Medical Test
evidence.snippet:
ep into the skin 
Is larger than 3 inches across 
Looks leathery and dry 
Has patches of white, brown, or black 
Swells very fast 
Gets more painful over time, has pus, or causes a fever , which may be signs of an infection 
Also, get medical help for all burns that:
Are caused by electricity or chemicals 
Are on the face, hands, genitals, buttocks, over a joint, or go all the way around a part of the body, such as a leg or the chest 
Happen with inhalation (breathing in) of smoke, hot gasses, or chemicals 
Happen to a child under age 5 or an adult older than age 70 
References 
Agrawal A, Raibagkar SC, Vora HJ. Friction Burns: Epidemiology and Prevention. Ann Burns Fire Disasters [Internet]. 2008 Mar 31 [cited 2023 Mar 8]; 21(1): 3-6. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3188131 
American Burn Association [Internet]. Chicago (IL): American Burn Association; c2022

Conditions A to Z
Cystitis 
Cystitis is a urinary tract infection (UTI) that affects the bladder. It's common, particularly in women. It often gets better by itself, but may sometimes be treated with antibiotics. 
Some people get cystitis frequently and may need regular or long-term treatment.
Symptoms of cystitis 
Symptoms of cystitis include:
pain, burning or stinging when you pee needing to pee more often and urgently than usual pee that's dark, cloudy or strong smelling pain low down in your tummy Symptoms in young children may also include:
a high temperature – they feel hotter than usual if you touch their neck, back or tummy wetting themselves reduced appetite and being s
